content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My little brother ___ ride a bike, but he needs help to start.
distractor_rationale: could refers to past ability, but the sentence describes a current skill with present tense context
will can is never grammatically possible; the correct future form is will be able to
is able is incomplete; the correct form requires is able to before the verb

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Maria ___ speak any English when she started school last year.
distractor_rationale: can't refers to present inability, but last year clearly places the situation in the past
isn't able to is a present form and contradicts the past time marker last year
won't be able to refers to future inability, which is impossible with the past marker last year

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ___ to play the guitar, but he practises every day now.
distractor_rationale: can't refers to present inability, but the contrast with now shows the situation is in the past
wasn't able is incomplete; the correct form requires wasn't able to before the verb
will can't is never grammatically possible in English

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: After months of practice, Sara ___ finally play the whole song without stopping.
distractor_rationale: could is not used for a specific achieved result in the past; was able to is required for a single successful past achievement
can refers to present ability, but after months of practice and finally show this is a completed past event
will be able to refers to future ability, which contradicts the past context of after months of practice

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: James has been studying hard, so he ___ pass his driving test next month.
distractor_rationale: will can is never grammatically possible; can cannot follow will
could refers to past ability or polite possibility, not a future result of current preparation
can expresses present ability, but next month clearly places the situation in the future

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: When I was young, I ___ run very fast, but now my knees hurt too much.
distractor_rationale: can refers to present ability, which contradicts when I was young and now my knees hurt
was able to describes a specific single achievement in the past; could is correct for a general past ability
will be able to refers to future ability, which is impossible with the past time marker when I was young

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The rescue team searched for hours and ___ find all the missing hikers before dark.
distractor_rationale: could is not used for a specific successful outcome in a past situation; was able to is required here
can refers to present ability, which is impossible given the past narrative context
were able is incomplete; the correct form requires were able to before the verb

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: By the end of this course, students ___ write a formal email without making basic grammar mistakes.
distractor_rationale: will can is never grammatically possible; can cannot be used after will
could refers to past ability or polite possibility, not a future outcome of a course
are able to describes a current ability, but by the end of this course places the result in the future

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Although the traffic was very heavy that morning, Anna ___ arrive at the interview on time.
distractor_rationale: could is not used for a single specific successful achievement in a past situation; was able to is required
can refers to present ability, which contradicts the past time markers that morning and was
had been able to implies the ability existed before another past event, but no such earlier reference point is given in the sentence

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: David has damaged his right hand, so he ___ type at work until it heals completely.
distractor_rationale: couldn't refers to past inability, but has damaged is present perfect indicating a current situation affecting the present
won't can is never grammatically possible in English
wasn't able to refers to past inability, which contradicts the present perfect has damaged and the present context

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My little brother ___ ride a bike now, but he couldn't do it last year.
distractor_rationale: could refers to past ability, but the contrast with 'last year' shows this is a present ability
'will can' is not a grammatical form in English; will be able to is the correct future construction
'is able' is incomplete without 'to ride'; the bare infinitive after 'is able' is ungrammatical

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: When Sara was seven, she ___ speak two languages fluently.
distractor_rationale: 'can' refers to present ability and cannot be used to describe ability in a past time frame marked by 'when Sara was seven'
'will be able to' refers to future ability, which contradicts the past time marker 'when Sara was seven'
'is able to' is present tense and cannot describe a past state established by 'when Sara was seven'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: After months of practice, Tom ___ finally play the guitar well enough to perform at the school concert.
distractor_rationale: 'could' describes general past ability, but this sentence describes a specific achievement reached after effort; 'was able to' is required for a specific past accomplishment
'can' is present tense and contradicts the past context established by 'after months of practice' and 'the school concert'
'will be able to' refers to future ability and contradicts the completed past event described in the sentence

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If you keep studying every day, you ___ read the news in Spanish before the end of the year.
distractor_rationale: 'will can' is ungrammatical; English does not allow two modal verbs together, so 'will be able to' is the correct future ability form
'could' describes past ability or polite possibility, not a future outcome following a present condition
'can' describes present ability, but the future time marker 'before the end of the year' and the conditional 'if you keep studying' require a future form

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Despite the heavy traffic on the road, the ambulance ___ reach the hospital in time to save the patient.
distractor_rationale: 'could' is used for general past ability, not for a specific successful achievement against difficult circumstances; 'was able to' correctly expresses that this particular effort succeeded
'couldn't' means the ambulance failed to reach the hospital, which contradicts 'in time to save the patient' and reverses the meaning of the sentence
'can' is present tense and is incompatible with the completed past event described by the context of the sentence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: My little brother ___ ride a bike now — he learned last summer.
distractor_rationale: could refers to past ability, but the sentence says 'now', indicating present ability
will can is ungrammatical — can cannot follow will; will be able to is the correct future form
is able is incomplete — the correct form is is able to, and even then 'can' is the natural present ability form here

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: When Maria was three years old, she ___ say a few words in two languages.
distractor_rationale: can is a present tense modal and cannot refer to past ability at age three
will be able to refers to future ability, not a past childhood situation
can to is ungrammatical — can is never followed by to before the infinitive

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom ___ speak French very well — he takes classes every week.
distractor_rationale: could signals past ability, but the present tense clue 'takes classes every week' shows this is a current ability
can to is ungrammatical — can is a bare modal and is never followed by to
will can is ungrammatical — two modal verbs cannot appear together in English

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: After months of practice, Sara ___ finally play the piano piece from start to finish without any mistakes.
distractor_rationale: could is not used for a specific single achievement in the past; was able to is required when someone succeeded on one particular occasion
can is a present modal and cannot describe a completed past achievement
will be able to refers to future ability, not a past accomplishment that has already happened

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Once James finishes his driving lessons next month, he ___ drive to work on his own.
distractor_rationale: will can is ungrammatical — can cannot follow will; will be able to is the only correct future ability form
could refers to past ability or polite possibility, not a future ability that will result from finishing lessons
can describes present ability, but the time marker 'once he finishes next month' places the ability in the future

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The doctor told Anna that she ___ return to work after two weeks of rest.
distractor_rationale: could here would suggest past ability or present polite possibility, not a future ability reported in indirect speech after a past-tense reporting verb
will be able to is incorrect in reported speech after a past tense verb; back-shift requires would be able to
can is a present modal and does not fit the reported speech context introduced by the past tense 'told'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: David ___ swim when he was young, but he has forgotten most of what he learned.
distractor_rationale: can describes present ability, which contradicts the past time marker 'when he was young'
was able to is used for a specific past achievement on one occasion, not for a general past ability over a period of time
will be able to refers to future ability, not a past general skill that has since been lost

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Even though the fire had spread quickly through the building, the firefighters ___ rescue everyone inside before it collapsed.
distractor_rationale: could is not used for a specific successful achievement in the past on one occasion; were able to is required to express that they actually succeeded
can is a present modal and cannot describe a completed past event in a building fire that already happened
would be able to expresses a conditional or reported future ability, not a specific past achievement that is presented as a fact

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Lisa has been practising her presentation every day this week, so she ___ deliver it confidently by Friday.
distractor_rationale: will can is ungrammatical — modal verbs cannot be stacked; the correct future ability form is will be able to
could signals past ability or polite possibility, not an expected future ability resulting from current preparation
was able to describes a specific past achievement that has already occurred, but the time marker 'by Friday' places the ability in the future

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The technician worked on the broken server for three hours and ___ fix the problem before the office opened in the morning.
distractor_rationale: could is inappropriate for a specific single past achievement where success is confirmed; was able to is required to show the technician actually succeeded on that occasion
can is a present modal and cannot describe a completed past action that happened before the office opened
will be able to refers to future ability, but the sentence describes a completed event in the past confirmed by 'worked for three hours'

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: My little brother can ride a bike when he was six years old.
content.error_sentence: My little brother can ride a bike when he was six years old.
content.corrected_sentence: My little brother could ride a bike when he was six years old.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She can to speak three languages, which is very impressive.
content.error_sentence: She can to speak three languages, which is very impressive.
content.corrected_sentence: She can speak three languages, which is very impressive.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: After practising every day for a month, Tom could finally finish the difficult puzzle last Sunday.
content.error_sentence: After practising every day for a month, Tom could finally finish the difficult puzzle last Sunday.
content.corrected_sentence: After practising every day for a month, Tom was finally able to finish the difficult puzzle last Sunday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: David will can join the advanced class next term if he passes the test.
content.error_sentence: David will can join the advanced class next term if he passes the test.
content.corrected_sentence: David will be able to join the advanced class next term if he passes the test.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The technician could fix the broken printer yesterday after he found the right part.
content.error_sentence: The technician could fix the broken printer yesterday after he found the right part.
content.corrected_sentence: The technician was able to fix the broken printer yesterday after he found the right part.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: He can to understand the instructions, so he assembled the furniture without any help.
content.error_sentence: He can to understand the instructions, so he assembled the furniture without any help.
content.corrected_sentence: He could understand the instructions, so he assembled the furniture without any help.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Although the storm was very strong, the rescue team could reach all the passengers safely that evening.
content.error_sentence: Although the storm was very strong, the rescue team could reach all the passengers safely that evening.
content.corrected_sentence: Although the storm was very strong, the rescue team was able to reach all the passengers safely that evening.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Once she completes the training programme next month, she will can handle customer complaints on her own.
content.error_sentence: Once she completes the training programme next month, she will can handle customer complaints on her own.
content.corrected_sentence: Once she completes the training programme next month, she will be able to handle customer complaints on her own.
